result.question:
Assess whether the SESTM news-sentiment equity strategy (Ke, Kelly & Xiu, "Predicting Returns with Text Data," Journal of Financial Economics 2021 / NBER Working Paper 26186) retains a TRADABLE, net-of-cost edge for a SMALL-to-MEDIUM book (roughly $1M-$500M AUM), versus its headline GROSS results. Cite primary sources and any replications.

CONTEXT (self-contained): SESTM's reported EQUAL-WEIGHTED long-short spread earns a GROSS annualized Sharpe ~4.29, but with ~95% DAILY turnover and returns concentrated in SMALL/ILLIQUID stocks; the VALUE-WEIGHTED Sharpe is much lower (~1.33). Orthogonality to price factors (long-short spread <=10% R-squared on Fama-French) is a covariance property and is NOT in question — the open question is NET PROFITABILITY and CAPACITY after real-world frictions. The system that would adopt this currently trades a ~30-name LARGE-CAP universe and has NO alternative-data feed today (price-only).

Find evidence on:
1. NET-OF-COST results — does the original paper or any replication report Sharpe/returns AFTER transaction costs, bid-ask spread, and market impact? Quantify the equal-weighted vs value-weighted gap and what it implies about WHERE the alpha lives (microcaps vs liquid names).
2. TURNOVER & COST DRAG — for ~95% daily turnover, what round-trip cost (in bps) erases the edge? Give a Fermi / break-even estimate: at what per-trade cost does a gross Sharpe ~4.29 (and the more realistic value-weighted ~1.33) decay toward ~1.0 and toward zero?
3. CAPACITY — academic estimates of news-sentiment / high-turnover anomaly capacity; how AUM scales market-impact cost; whether the edge survives EXCLUDING microcaps (e.g., bottom NYSE size deciles) or under liquidity screens.
4. DECAY — post-publication and out-of-sample decay of text-sentiment anomalies (McLean & Pontiff 2016 ~26-58% decay; any SESTM-specific extension after the 2017 sample end).
5. IMPLEMENTATION FEASIBILITY for a small book with no current alt-data: minimum-viable news data sources (RavenPack, Tiingo news, Alpha Vantage news sentiment, GDELT, free EDGAR 8-K text) and whether a LOWER-TURNOVER, LIQUID-UNIVERSE variant (weekly rebalance, large-cap only) preserves a meaningful fraction of the edge.
6. ALTERNATIVES — does a simpler, lower-turnover FinBERT or dictionary (Loughran-McDonald) sentiment on a liquid large-cap universe capture a tradable slice, suitable for a ~30-name large-cap book?

CRITICALLY distinguish gross vs net throughout, and flag claims that rest only on gross, high-turnover, microcap-concentrated results. Conclude with a NET-OF-COST VERDICT: is SESTM tradable for a small/medium book, tradable ONLY in microcaps, or effectively gross-only? — plus the minimum-viable, lowest-cost way to test a liquid large-cap variant.
result.summary: SESTM's headline edge is GROSS, microcap-concentrated, and high-turnover — and it does not survive intact into the liquid large-cap, price-only regime this 30-name book actually trades. The equal-weighted long-short Sharpe of 4.29 (GROSS, ~94.7% daily turnover) collapses to a value-weighted GROSS Sharpe of 1.33 (~91.6% turnover), the textbook signature of alpha living in small/illiquid names. The authors' OWN net-of-cost analysis (10bps/day per Frazzini et al. 2018) shows net survival is only achievable by re-engineering turnover down: net Sharpe PEAKS at 2.30 (gamma=0.5, turnover cut to 0.46) and falls to 1.17 at gamma=0.1 — naively applying costs to the 94%-turnover original drives net toward zero. The broader cost literature is decisive on the mechanism: high-turnover anomalies generally fail to produce significant NET spreads (Novy-Marx & Velikov), and the one high-turnover anomaly studied by Frazzini-Israel-Moskowitz (short-term reversal, the directional turnover analog) is the only one that does not survive costs ($9.5B US break-even vs $52-103B for momentum/value/size). Critically, McLean & Pontiff show post-publication decay is WORST precisely in liquid large-cap names (the cheapest to arbitrage) — undercutting the assumption that a liquid large-cap variant preserves the edge. NET-OF-COST VERDICT: for this book's large-cap, price-only universe SESTM is effectively GROSS-ONLY (no confirmed evidence demonstrates a net-of-cost, post-decay, large-cap-only tradable slice); the genuine residual edge is microcap-concentrated — reachable by a $1M-$500M book's AUM (capacity is NOT the binding constraint at this size) but not by its stated large-cap universe.

findings.claim: SESTM's headline edge is GROSS and microcap-concentrated: equal-weighted long-short Sharpe is 4.29 at ~94.7% daily turnover, but the value-weighted (large-cap-tilted) version earns only Sharpe 1.33 at ~91.6% turnover. Both figures are GROSS of all transaction costs; the EW->VW collapse (and the small-stock first-15-min response of 52bps vs 11bps for large stocks) shows the alpha lives in small/illiquid names and shrinks sharply when capital weights toward liquid large caps. The authors explicitly state the headline tables ignore transaction costs and were reported to show predictive content, not net profitability.
findings.evidence: Primary source verified (NBER WP 26186, Ke/Kelly/Xiu, JFE 2021). Table 4 read verbatim: SESTM EW Sharpe 4.29 / turnover 94.7% / 33bps per day; VW Sharpe 1.33 / turnover 91.6% / 10bps per day. Headline 4.3 EW Sharpe confirmed p.29. Section 3.8 states verbatim: 'Our trading strategy performance analysis thus far ignores transaction costs... The profitability of the trading strategy net of costs is neither here nor there for assessing sentiment predictability... the practical viability of our sentiment strategies is difficult to ascertain from preceding tables due to their large turnover.' SCOPING NOTE (claim 3 was 1-1): 'thus far' scopes only the gross headline; the paper SEPARATELY presents a net analysis (Table 5) — so 'headline ignores costs' must NOT be read as 'authors concede not net-profitable.' Microcap concentration corroborated by the paper's own size analysis (small-stock 52bps vs large-stock 11bps first-15-min response).

findings.claim: Net of cost, SESTM's edge survives only after deliberate turnover engineering, and at a large fraction below the gross headline. Applying the authors' own 10bps/day cost (which they say approximates large asset managers' costs per Frazzini et al. 2018), the turnover-constrained variant's NET Sharpe PEAKS at 2.30 (gamma=0.5, turnover cut from ~0.95 to 0.46) and falls to 1.17 at the most-restricted gamma=0.1; max net return is ~13.7bps/day. Net is positive but ~54% of gross at best — and only because turnover was re-engineered down. Naively applying 10bps to the original 94%-turnover EW strategy would drive net Sharpe near zero.
findings.evidence: Verified line-by-line against the primary PDF. Sec 3.8: 'we assume that each portfolio incurs a daily transaction cost of 10bps. The choice of 10bps approximates the average trading cost experienced by large asset managers, as reported in Frazzini et al. (2018)... the net Sharpe ratio peaks at 2.3 when gamma = 0.5.' Table 5 (EWCT, net of 10bps): gamma=0.5 -> turnover 0.46, gross Sharpe 4.26, NET Sharpe 2.30; gamma=0.1 -> turnover 0.08, NET Sharpe 1.17; max net daily return 13.74bps (gamma=0.9). Key nuance (cuts TOWARD the finding): the 2.30 net peak is a re-engineered low-turnover portfolio (EWCT), NOT the 4.3 strategy run net-of-cost — so 2.30 is the OPTIMISTIC net figure, achievable only after turnover engineering. The authors' broader abstract claim of net survival (weekly EW net Sharpe ~4.0, decaying to ~0.9 quarterly) rests on a gentler flat-cost model and small-stock concentration.

findings.claim: High-turnover strategies are the most cost-fragile category and generally fail to produce statistically significant NET-of-cost spreads — the structural reason SESTM's ~95% daily turnover is a red flag for net profitability. Among standard anomalies, short-term reversal (STR, the directional turnover analog to SESTM) is the ONLY one that does not survive real-world institutional trading costs: break-even fund size ~$9.5B US / ~$8-13B globally and net return just 1.52%/yr (t=2.14, marginal) at an implied $1.5B size, versus $52-103B (US) / $89-190B (global) break-evens for momentum/value/size. Capacity is inversely related to turnover. Separately, anomalies with one-sided MONTHLY turnover below 50% generally retain significant net spreads while higher-turnover ones do not.
findings.evidence: Frazzini-Israel-Moskowitz 'Trading Costs of Asset Pricing Anomalies' (RFS 2018; ~$1T live institutional trades, 19 markets, 1998-2011), verified verbatim: break-even fund sizes size/value/momentum = $103/$83/$52B US, $156/$190/$89B global; 'Short-term reversal strategies, on the other hand, do not survive transactions costs at sizes greater than $9 billion in the U.S. or $13 billion globally'; STR net return 1.52%/yr (t=2.14) at implied $1.5B, break-even $9.51B; STR is highest turnover (~3.05/month) with negative in-sample net (-2.10%); authors conclude STR viability 'is not very compelling.' Novy-Marx & Velikov (RFS 2016, SSRN 2535173) verified verbatim: 'Most of the anomalies... with one-sided monthly turnover lower than 50% continue to generate statistically significant net spreads... Few of the strategies with higher turnover do.' IMPORTANT HEDGES: (1) STR is a turnover/capacity LOWER-BOUND analog, NOT turnover-matched — STR is ~300%/yr, SESTM ~24,000%/yr (~80x); FIM explicitly EXCLUDE intraday strategies. The analogy runs in the conservative direction (monthly STR already dies at $9.5B, so a daily signal is a fortiori more fragile). (2) The $9.5B break-even is LARGE relative to a $1M-$500M book, so for THIS book capacity is NOT the binding constraint — cost-fragility from turnover is. (3) NMV studied monthly-rebalanced anomalies; the daily extrapolation is conservative, not measured.

findings.claim: Post-publication decay materially discounts SESTM's in-sample (through ~2017) gross results AND specifically undercuts the liquid large-cap variant. Average post-publication anomaly decay is ~35% (2014 draft) / 58% (published JF 2016 version; 26% out-of-sample), attributed to statistical bias and price pressure from aware investors. Crucially, the decline is GREATEST for anomaly portfolios of large, liquid, high-dividend, low-idiosyncratic-risk stocks — i.e., the cheapest to arbitrage. So the most arbitrageable (liquid large-cap) slice is precisely where post-publication alpha decays most, while surviving alpha concentrates in hard-to-arbitrage (illiquid/microcap) names.
findings.evidence: McLean & Pontiff, 'Does Academic Research Destroy Stock Return Predictability?' Journal of Finance 71(1):5-32 (2016). Verified verbatim from full text: 'The average post-publication decay... is about 35%' (2014 working-paper draft, 82 characteristics); published JF version reports 'portfolio returns are 26% lower out-of-sample and 58% lower post-publication.' Cross-sectional finding verified verbatim: 'Consistent with costly (limited) arbitrage, the post-publication return decline is greater for anomaly portfolios that consist of stocks that are large, liquid, have high dividend yields, and have low idiosyncratic risk.' Mechanism corroborated by SESTM's own EW(4.29)>>VW(1.33) signature. CAVEAT: M&P's 'decline' is RELATIVE (scaled by in-sample mean), a cross-anomaly average over ~82-97 mostly accounting/price predictors with sample ending before SESTM's 2017 window — not a SESTM-specific measurement; the liquid slice can retain positive-but-smaller relative alpha. The surviving illiquid/microcap alpha is GROSS and largely untradable net for this book — so the finding if anything understates the net problem.

findings.claim: Methodology (decision-relevant context, not tradability): SESTM (Sentiment Extraction via Screening and Topic Modeling) is a supervised three-step text-mining method — (1) predictive screening of sentiment terms, (2) topic-model sentiment weighting, (3) penalized-likelihood article-level scoring — specifically adapted to return prediction and explicitly distinct from dictionary-based (e.g., Loughran-McDonald) or commercial-vendor (e.g., RavenPack) sentiment scores. Replicating it requires a labeled news feed and the supervised pipeline, not an off-the-shelf dictionary.
findings.evidence: NBER WP 26186 abstract verbatim: method 'proceeds in three steps: 1) isolating a list of sentiment terms via predictive screening, 2) assigning sentiment weights to these words via topic modeling, and 3) aggregating terms into an article-level sentiment score via penalized likelihood.' And: 'Unlike more common sentiment scores used for stock return prediction (e.g., those sold by commercial vendors or built with dictionary-based methods), our supervised learning framework constructs a sentiment score that is specifically adapted to the problem of return prediction.' Closely related to LDA but trained supervised. Purely descriptive — makes no tradability/capacity/turnover claim, so gross-vs-net caveats do not bear on it. Decision-relevance: low (does not establish a net edge), but it defines what a faithful replication would have to build.

result.caveats: GROSS vs NET labeling (task mandate): 4.29 EW and 1.33 VW Sharpe are GROSS; 2.30 and 1.17 are NET but only for a turnover-ENGINEERED variant at 10bps/day; $9.5B is an institutional-cost break-even (a low-cost arbitrageur's figure, NOT achievable by a small book lacking such execution). THE LOAD-BEARING ABSENCE: no confirmed claim measures a net-of-cost, post-decay, LARGE-CAP-ONLY SESTM variant. The verdict therefore must NOT infer a net large-cap number from the gross 1.33 (no costs applied) or from the EW-inclusive net 2.30 (which uses small stocks). Conflating those would be the single error that undercuts the report. CAPACITY IS NOT THE BINDING CONSTRAINT at $1M-$500M: STR's $9.5B break-even is far above this book's AUM, so a small book is small enough to actually reach the microcaps where the edge lives — the binding constraint is turnover-driven cost-fragility plus the large-cap universe being the wrong place for the residual alpha. ANALOGY HEDGE: STR (~300%/yr, price-reversal) is a directional/LOWER-BOUND analog to SESTM (~24,000%/yr, news-sentiment), not turnover-matched; FIM exclude intraday strategies. The analogy runs conservatively (monthly STR dies at $9.5B -> daily SESTM more fragile) but is not a SESTM-specific net measurement. DECAY caveat: McLean-Pontiff decay is a RELATIVE cross-anomaly average ending before SESTM's 2017 window, not a SESTM-specific extension. ITEMS WITH NO CONFIRMED-CLAIM SUPPORT (do not treat as findings): research-question item 5 (data-source feasibility/cost for RavenPack/Tiingo/Alpha Vantage/GDELT/EDGAR) and item 6 (FinBERT/Loughran-McDonald net tradable large-cap slice) have NO confirmed claim; the only adjacent claim (an LLM/OPT-GPT3 news strategy, arxiv 2412.19245) was REFUTED 0-3 as gross-only. The minimum-viable-test recommendation below is synthesis beyond the verified evidence. MINIMUM-VIABLE, LOWEST-COST TEST (synthesis, unverified): before buying any paid feed, test a WEEKLY-rebalance, VALUE-WEIGHTED, LARGE-CAP-ONLY variant on a free/cheap text source (EDGAR 8-K text or Alpha Vantage news sentiment) using a simple Loughran-McDonald dictionary first; measure NET-of-cost (round-trip ~20-40bps assumed) Sharpe with explicit turnover reporting; only escalate to a supervised SESTM pipeline + paid feed if the cheap dictionary variant clears a pre-registered net floor. Expectation given the evidence: a large-cap-only net edge is unlikely to be large, because that is exactly where decay is worst and where the gross signal is weakest.
openQuestions: Is there any SESTM-SPECIFIC net-of-cost result restricted to a LIQUID LARGE-CAP universe (no microcaps), under realistic small-book round-trip costs (~20-40bps) and a weekly/large-cap rebalance? No confirmed claim measures this — it is the exact gap blocking a tradability verdict for this 30-name book.

Has SESTM been replicated or extended OUT-OF-SAMPLE after the 2017 sample end (e.g., 2018-2025), and does the value-weighted/large-cap GROSS edge (1.33) survive both post-publication decay and net-of-cost frictions in that period?

What is the realistic NET-of-cost performance of a simpler, lower-turnover FinBERT or Loughran-McDonald dictionary sentiment strategy on a large-cap-only universe — does any peer-reviewed replication show a tradable slice suitable for a ~30-name large-cap book? (The only adjacent evidence was refuted as gross-only.)

For a small book that CAN reach microcaps (capacity is not binding at $1M-$500M), what is the realistic NET edge of the microcap-concentrated EW variant after small-book (non-institutional) execution costs and borrow constraints on the short leg — i.e., is the residual edge tradable in microcaps in practice, or only on paper?

refuted.claim: The authors explicitly exclude high-frequency/intra-day strategies from their cost estimates because their cost separability (portfolio formation independent of execution) breaks down when portfolio weights and trading are determined simultaneously and endogenously — exactly the regime a daily-turnover news-sentiment strategy occupies.

News-driven price response is far larger and slower in small/illiquid and high-volatility stocks (52bps vs 11bps for large in first 15 min; 59bps vs 12bps for high- vs low-vol), implying the tradable edge lives precisely where market-impact costs are highest and a 30-name large-cap book would capture the weakest signal.

Measured per-trade trading costs are far smaller than prior academic estimates: mean market impact 12.18 bps (median 7.98 bps; value-weighted 18.65 bps) and mean implementation shortfall 13 bps. The authors find real-world costs are 'approximately one tenth as large' as the literature, so model-based cost assumptions overstate the drag. For a Fermi break-even on a high-turnover book, these one-way figures (~8-20 bps) imply a round-trip of roughly ~16-40 bps for a low-cost institution — and materially higher for a small book lacking such execution.

An LLM-based (OPT/GPT-3) long-short news-sentiment strategy on US financial news (2010-2023) earns a GROSS daily long-short Sharpe of 3.05; the paper reports NO net-of-cost, turnover, or transaction-cost figures in its abstract, so the headline edge is gross-only.

Realized round-trip cost drag on the highest-turnover strategy (STR) was 6.75% per year, far exceeding its break-even cost of 8.27% only by a thin margin and producing a NEGATIVE realized net return — a concrete Fermi anchor that for high-turnover, frequently-rebalanced strategies, per-period frictions consume nearly all (or more than all) of the gross edge.

Transaction costs reduce every strategy's profitability and statistical significance without exception, so any gross-only result (including SESTM's gross Sharpe) overstates achievable net performance and raises data-snooping concerns.

The primary source claims its method 'excels at extracting return-predictive signals,' but the NBER landing page reports the claim only qualitatively — it provides no net-of-cost, turnover, capacity, or equal- vs value-weighted Sharpe figures, so the headline gross Sharpe ~4.29 / VW ~1.33 and ~95% turnover figures in the research question cannot be verified from this page and require the full PDF or published JFE version.

The standard net-return identity is Net = Gross − 2 × (each-leg turnover) × (bid-ask spread); with the typical anomaly's 15% per-leg monthly turnover, a ~100 bps post-publication spread fully erases a 30 bps/month gross return, taking net to ~0 bps. This is the exact break-even Fermi the research question requests: at ~15%/leg turnover, a round-trip cost of ~100 bps (per leg, paid twice) zeroes a 30 bps/month edge.

For the modal equal-weighted long-short quintile construction used by the vast majority of anomaly papers, trading costs collapse an impressive 66 bps/month in-sample gross return to just 5 bps net in-sample, and to NEGATIVE -3 bps/month net post-publication — i.e. gross-only results do not survive as net profits.

The alpha lives in illiquid/microcap names: equal-weighted anomaly portfolios load on the right tail of the spread distribution, paying a mean ~67 bps spread in 2014 (>4x the 16 bps NYSE average). Value-weighting cuts costs precisely by shrinking microcaps to a tiny portfolio weight — directly implying a liquid/value-weighted variant sacrifices most of the gross edge.

Even with optimized cost mitigation (value-weighting plus a buy/hold 'banding' rule that lowers turnover), post-publication net returns are tiny: 4–13 bps/month on average, and restricting to value-weighting yields only 4–7 bps/month; the strongest cost-optimized anomalies net just 10–20 bps post-publication. A lower-turnover, liquid variant preserves only a small fraction of the headline gross edge.

sources.angle: broad/primary — original paper net-of-cost & EW vs VW
